body:
Parent: #651

## Motivation and evidence

Across roughly fifteen closed issues (#481–#508 range and earlier, e.g. #67, #292, #326), the maintainer's requirement "**all languages**" was silently narrowed to the four seed languages (en/ru/hi/zh). The vision (`VISION.md`, issue #526 contract) states that translation goes through the language-neutral meta language, so once meanings are grounded in Wikidata P/Q anchors, **adding a language should reduce to data**: the destination-language labels/lexemes on the same anchors. That property has never been demonstrated for a fifth language.

Open issue #660 (E41) builds the bulk importer for the existing four languages. This issue owns the **any-language protocol**: the repeatable, data-only procedure that takes the system from N to N+1 languages, and the contract that keeps every added language honest.

## Requirements

1. **Language-addition protocol as data + docs**: a documented, scripted procedure (building on #660's importer) that adds a new language by importing its labels/lexemes for the existing grounded meaning set, its detection rules, its multilingual-response entries, and its operation-vocabulary surfaces — with **zero Rust changes** (any needed code change is a defect to fix, per the "adding a language is a seed-data edit" precedent of E33/#326).
2. **Round-trip contract per language** (issue #526): language→meta→same-language survival tests and directed-pair tests through the shared meaning are generated automatically for every registered language, not hand-written per pair.
3. **Prove it with a fifth language**: add one new language end to end (candidate: Spanish or Arabic — decide in the case study from Wikidata lexeme coverage data) and pass the same specification suites the four seed languages pass (greetings, identity, concept lookup, math wrappers, operation vocabulary, translation round-trip).
4. **Honest coverage ledger**: `data/seed/languages.lino` records per-language coverage (meanings with labels, detection confidence, suites passing); partially covered languages answer in-language where covered and state the gap honestly otherwise (`language_gap` event) instead of silently falling back to English.
5. **Scale path**: document (and bound) what full breadth would take — Wikidata supports hundreds of languages; the protocol must be repeatable by a data contributor without Rust knowledge.

## Acceptance criteria

- The fifth language passes the auto-generated round-trip matrix and ≥ 80% of the multilingual specification suite, with the remainder recorded as `language_gap` entries.
- A dry-run of the protocol on a sixth language produces a coverage report without any code edit.
- CI language-parity checks (the guard konard requested ~11 times across PRs #175–#240) extend automatically to every language registered in the ledger.

## Dependencies

- Blocked by #660 (E41 importer is the ingestion engine).
- Related: #659 (E40 hardcoded-string lint prevents regressions), #674 (E55 same-skill-links-from-any-language), #526/#292 (closed parents).

## Process

Collect data to `docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` (Wikidata lexeme coverage statistics per candidate language, detection-rule research, survey: Universal Dependencies, CLDR); single PR until every requirement is addressed.
title: E64: Any-language protocol — grow beyond en/ru/hi/zh by data-only addition with an auto-generated round-trip contract

body:
## Issue #531 — Pattern inference (1D sequences/text, 2D grids)

Closes #531.

This PR ships an **implementation** of pattern inference on a self-contained,
link-native sequence substrate, ported in spirit from
[`linksplatform/Data.Doublets.Sequences`](https://github.com/linksplatform/Data.Doublets.Sequences)
and grounded in the links meta-theory. It began as the requested research and
proposal pass and is now followed, in the same PR, by the runtime implementation
wired into the solver — with tests, ontology, and traceable requirements.

### What was built

**Link-native sequence substrate (`src/sequences/`)**
- `store.rs` — a doublet store where every link is a `(source, target)` pair;
  points are self-referential links, composites are structurally deduplicated,
  and `expand()` gives a lossless round-trip.
- `symbols.rs` — unique symbol/point allocation for atoms.
- `converter.rs` — a balanced sequence→tree converter with a sequence index and
  an adjacent-pair frequency cache.
- `compression.rs` — Re-Pair-style **associative deduplication**: repeatedly
  replace the most frequent adjacent pair, keep an auditable trace, and expand
  losslessly back to the original.

**Pattern inference (`src/sequences/{patterns_1d,grid_2d,inference}.rs`)**
- 1D: repetition, period, palindrome, reversal, translation, and classification.
- 2D: horizontal / vertical / diagonal symmetry, 90/180/270 rotations,
  reflections, translations, and the invariant-transform set.
- `inference.rs` unifies these into reports (`infer_sequence_patterns`,
  `infer_grid_patterns`) that also run the compression pass.

**Solver integration (`src/solver_handlers/pattern_inference.rs`)**
- A data-gated `pattern_inference` method: it fires only when a prompt both
  signals pattern intent *and* carries a parseable run of ≥3 atoms or a grid.
- Ranked ahead of `concept_lookup` in intent formalization via a routing mirror
  (`looks_like_pattern_inference`), so `what is the pattern in this grid?` with
  data is analysed structurally, while a bare `what is a pattern?` still routes to
  the concept lookup.
- Emits default-off `pattern_inference:*` diagnostics to the event log.
- **Localized into every seeded language (en, ru, hi, zh):** a response-language
  follow-up (issue #556 — e.g. "answer in Russian") replays a prior
  pattern-inference answer through the solver and re-renders the full report —
  classification, counts, dedup/compression, next-element prediction, and grid
  symmetry labels — in the requested language via
  `try_pattern_inference_with_response_language`, instead of stranding it in
  English. English delegates to the substrate `summary()` so its wording stays
  byte-identical.

**Ontology (`data/seed/concepts.lino`)**
- Seed concepts for sequence, pattern, repetition, compression, deduplication,
  symmetry, rotation, reflection, translation, analogy, invariant, and
  transformation — rooted in links and closed by the total-closure resolver.

### How to reproduce / try it

```
cargo run --example issue_531_pattern_inference_demo
```

Example — `solve("find the pattern in 1 2 1 2 1 2")` reports a repetition of a
2-element block ×3, deduplicates to 50% (lossless), and predicts next = `1`.
`solve("what is the pattern in this grid?\n1 2 1\n3 4 3")` reports a 2×3 grid
symmetric under a left-right mirror.

### Tests

- `tests/unit/sequences_{store,symbols,converter,compression,patterns_1d,grid_2d,inference}.rs`
  — unit coverage for the substrate and inference (externalized from `src/` to
  satisfy the source/test-placement CI gate).
- `tests/unit/issue_531_pattern_inference.rs` — end-to-end routing through
  `formal_ai::solve`, including the grid/definition disambiguation and the
  response-language localization of both the sequence and grid reports into
  every seeded language (en, ru, hi, zh).
- `tests/unit/issue_531_concepts_probe.rs` — the ontology resolves.
- `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_531.rs` — keeps the case study and
  `REQUIREMENTS.md` traceable.

Full suite: **1484 passing, 0 failing**. `cargo fmt` and `clippy` clean.

### Requirements traceability

`REQUIREMENTS.md` R402–R405 and `docs/case-studies/issue-531/requirements.md`
R531-05..14 are updated from *planned* to *implemented*, pointing at the shipped
modules and tests.
title: Implement pattern inference over link-native sequences (1D/2D) — issue #531

body:
The best translation is the translation that survives round trip translation.

So we should by default find such formulation for translation, that is more likely and in most part will survive round trip translation.

We need to make sure that all our logic related to formal translation via meta language (between programming languages, between natural languages) all have round trip translation quality test.

Translation to meta language from any other language must have zero data loss, meaning, roundtrip from meta language and back must work always.

Yet we also need to test roundtrips from Rust to meta language to JavaScript (roundtrip via meta language between JavaScript and Rust), from JavaScript to meta language to Rust (and round trip). The same for Russian -> meta language -> English, English -> meta language -> Russian. So Rust <-> JavaScript, Russian <-> English and so on. We should support tests for all roundtrips between each separate language and meta language, and between each 2 languages we support via meta language.

We should never support direct translation without meta language, as doing so will be computationally unsolvable.

For example if we have 100 languages, translators between them will be 100 * 100 = 10 000, and if we have 100 languages + meta language, we will need only 200 translators (to and from meta language from each 100 languages).

Make sure our vision, requirements, roadmap and contributing guide all do not contradict this, and all include all relevant text about the case.

We need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).

Please plan and execute everything in this single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally done.
title: Translation quality test

body:
Parent: #651. Full planning context with audits: `docs/case-studies/issue-651/` (this issue is E42 in `proposed-issues.md` there; added by PR #652).


**Problem**

REQUIREMENTS R384: the universal meta algorithm should formalize every
message as a probability-weighted statement and warn about contradictory
requirements with proposed resolutions. Today the method registry (#559) and
self-AST slice exist, and probability evidence (`src/probability.rs`) can
bias formalization — but statements do not carry explicit posterior weights
in the trace, and contradictions between user requirements are not detected
or surfaced.

**Approach**

1. Extend the formalization step to append a `statement_weight` link per
   accepted interpretation (posterior from the existing symbolic evidence +
   temperature machinery) so every formalized statement is inspectable as a
   weighted claim.
2. Add a contradiction detector over the requirement store: when a new
   formalized requirement conflicts with a retained one (same subject,
   incompatible predicate values — the `bank_river`/`bank_money` split
   machinery generalized to requirements), append a
   `requirement_contradiction` event.
3. Surface the warning in the reply with both statements, their weights, and
   at least one proposed resolution (split meanings, retract one, scope by
   context), reusing the retraction protocol.
4. Use issue #651's own mixed scope as a fixture (the issue text itself
   contains overlapping asks — the R384 note already suggests this).

**Existing components**

- `src/probability.rs`, `src/translation/selection.rs` — posterior scoring.
- `src/dreaming/learning.rs::LearnedRequirement` — retained requirements to
  check against.
- `src/event_log.rs` retraction protocol; meaning-split precedent in
  `src/seed/meanings.rs`.

**Acceptance criteria**

- `cargo test weighted_formalization` — every formalized statement in a
  multi-interpretation prompt carries a `statement_weight` link summing to 1
  across candidates.
- `cargo test requirement_contradiction` — feeding "always answer in
  Russian" then "never answer in Russian" yields a
  `requirement_contradiction` event and a reply containing both statements
  and a proposed resolution, in the prompt's language.
- Diagnostics stay default-off; weights appear in the trace, not the plain
  reply.
title: E42: Probability-weighted statement formalization with contradiction warnings (R384)

body:
Fixes #526

## Summary
- Reworked code translation (`translate_program`) to route through a language-neutral **code meta language** (`CodeMeaning` / `formalize_code_meaning` / `render_code_meaning`) instead of direct `(source, target)` match arms. This removes the N² anti-pattern issue #526 explicitly forbids: the translator now stays at `N` formalizers + `N` renderers, so pairs that never had a hardcoded arm (Python → JavaScript, Rust → Go) translate through one shared meaning for free.
- Added issue #526 round-trip translation quality requirements, solution notes, online research, and raw PR/issue snapshots under `docs/case-studies/issue-526/`.
- Expanded natural-language translation coverage so every supported language surface formalizes to the meta language and every directed en/ru/hi/zh pair round-trips through the shared meaning (already routed through `TranslationPipeline`, not direct pairs).
- Added Rust → JavaScript → Rust code translation coverage plus a `code_translation_routes_through_the_code_meta_language_not_direct_pairs` test that proves never-hardcoded pairs translate through one shared `meaning:` link.
- Updated `VISION.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `ROADMAP.md`, `REQUIREMENTS.md`, and `CONTRIBUTING.md` so future translation work is governed by the same round-trip, meta-language rule.

## Root obsolete practice fixed
`translate_program` previously matched on the concrete `(source, target)` language pair — a direct pair-specific translator. Issue #526 states direct translation without a meta language is computationally unsolvable (N languages → N² translators). The fix formalizes any source fragment into a `CodeMeaning` (source language irrelevant), then renders that meaning into the requested target; no `(source, target)` arm survives.

## Reproduction
Before the fix, the reproducer failed because Rust → JavaScript returned a translation gap instead of an add-function renderer, and cross pairs like Python → JavaScript were never supported:

```bash
cargo test --test unit rust_javascript_code_translation_round_trips_through_code_meaning -- --nocapture
cargo test --test unit code_translation_routes_through_the_code_meta_language_not_direct_pairs -- --nocapture
```

## Verification
Post-merge verification on branch `issue-526-215ae32f521b`:

```bash
cargo test --test unit translation_round_trip -- --nocapture
cargo test --test unit translation_via_links -- --nocapture
cargo test --test unit issue_526_translation_quality_documents_are_traceable -- --nocapture
cargo fmt --check
rust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features
cargo test
```

## Visuals
Not applicable; this change is tests, translation helper behavior, and documentation.
title: Add translation round-trip quality coverage

body:
Parent: #651

## Motivation and evidence

A repeatedly-stated pipeline was never delivered end to end: **translate all search results into the single meta language, merge them, and present the most relevant statements by deformalization**:

- Issue #505: exactly this ask — only web-search routing shipped.
- Issue #444: dynamic multi-source synthesis of how-to answers — not evidenced in PR #448.
- Issue #63: merge multi-language Wikipedia definitions of the same concept (deterministic fusion) with reversible translation learning — zero response at close; only the seed-concept fusion slice exists (R-issue-63 block).
- Issue #153/#180: Google-style normalized results (url + title + quote + "Read more"), dedup/joining across engines, source priority — konard noted #153 "was already ignored", and #180's restatement was also dropped.
- Issue #481: "reconstruct reasoning to the smallest atomic links substitution operations" for a Google-quality answer — only intent routing shipped.

This is the knowledge-side generality engine: answers built by fusing formalized statements from several sources, ranked by the symbolic probability layer, with every statement carrying provenance.

## Requirements

1. **Formalize every result**: each search hit / fetched source is converted into meta-language statements (reusing the formalization pipeline and the #563 resource-formalization boundary) with `source:` provenance per statement — not kept as opaque text snippets.
2. **Merge and dedupe by meaning**: statements from different sources/languages that formalize to the same meaning links are merged (one statement, N sources), increasing the statement's evidence weight under the relative-meta-logic tiers (original first sources highest, reposts ignored — the #535 policy).
3. **Rank and select**: the answer presents the most relevant fused statements by deformalization into the user's language, smallest-sufficient, with per-statement source chips; conflicting statements surface as recorded disagreements (`conflict:source_disagreement`) with both sides shown.
4. **Cross-language fusion**: a query answered poorly by sources in the user's language must exploit sources in other languages through the meta language (the #63 requirement) — demonstrated by a fixture where the decisive statement exists only in a non-query-language source.
5. **Normalized presentation**: the classic #153 asks — url + title + quoted supporting fragment + "read more" per source, dedup across engines — on web, CLI, and Telegram surfaces.
6. Determinism/offline: recorded search/fetch fixtures make the whole pipeline replayable in CI; live mode stays behind the existing `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API` gates.

## Acceptance criteria

- An open question with ≥ 3 cached sources yields an answer whose trace shows per-statement formalization, a merged statement with multi-source evidence, and ranked selection; repeated runs identical.
- The cross-language fixture (requirement 4) passes: the answer cites the foreign-language source and renders in the query language.
- A contradiction fixture shows both sides with tiers and posteriors instead of picking one silently.
- Presentation e2e on web + Telegram matches the normalized format.

## Dependencies

- Blocked by #661 (E42 statement weights) — the ranking substrate.
- Related: #660 (E41), world-models (contexts hold the fused statements), #687 (web search/fetch firing), closed parents #505/#444/#63/#153.

## Process

Collect data to `docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` (fusion design, RRF and source-tier research already partly in issue-444/issue-535 studies, survey: RDF named graphs provenance, TruthDiscovery literature — symbolic methods only); single PR until every requirement is addressed.
title: E67: Meta-language fusion of multi-source search results into ranked, provenance-carrying answers

Parent: #651. Full planning context with audits: `docs/case-studies/issue-651/` (this issue is E41 in `proposed-issues.md` there; added by PR #652).


**Problem**

Issue #538 produced richly-detailed meanings (grammatical number, part of
speech, bidirectional word ⇄ meaning links, Wikidata grounding) for a
curated set (tomato, potato) — by hand plus per-concept Agent-CLI recipes.
REQUIREMENTS R378 tracks the scale step: import such semantics **in bulk**
from external lexical sources so the network grows by data collection, not by
per-word engineering. The same pipeline is the natural home for two older
doc-only follow-ups: R282 (extend Wikidata grounding from the core meaning
set toward every seeded meaning) and the issue-#1-era REQUIREMENTS row about
chunked Wikipedia / Wikidata / Rosetta Code / Wikifunctions corpus import
jobs that was never scheduled.

**Approach**

1. Generalize `scripts/ground-meanings.rs` into `formal-ai import lexemes`:
   given a concept list (or a Wikidata SPARQL/lexeme dump slice), fetch
   lexeme forms per language (en/ru/hi/zh first), emit the enriched-surface
   `.lino` template the tomato block demonstrates, and write cache records
   under `data/cache/wikidata/`.
2. Respect the existing bounded-cache policy (`min(1%, 512)` per source) and
   `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API` gating: committed snapshots make tests offline.
3. Validate on import: every generated surface must parse, denote its
   meaning, carry `grammatical_number`/`part_of_speech` facets, and pass the
   grounding-closure tests that guard the tomato entry today.
4. Import a first batch (target: 100+ common nouns across 4 languages) and
   wire it into the seed so translation and formalization quality visibly
   improve (measure: formalization coverage on the benchmark prompts).

**Existing components**

- `scripts/ground-meanings.rs`, `src/seed/meanings.rs::WordForm`,
  `data/seed/meanings-translation.lino` — the shape to generate.
- `tests/unit/semantic_grounding.rs` closure tests — the validators.
- `src/knowledge.rs` cached-external-API discipline.

**Acceptance criteria**

- `formal-ai import lexemes --concepts <file> --offline` reproduces the
  committed batch byte-for-byte from cache (deterministic).
- `cargo test bulk_lexeme_import` covers: template emission, facet
  completeness, denotation bidirectionality, cache-record grounding.
- The seed grows by ≥ 100 grounded meanings with all existing grounding and
  translation tests green.
- The importer refuses to write entries that fail validation, recording
  `import_rejected` events instead.
title: E41: Bulk semantics importer from external lexical sources (R378)

body:
Parent: #651

## Motivation and evidence

Issue #559 mandated, in the strongest terms and twice: "Previous specialized logic must be removed… only memory + meta algorithm" — a full and total migration. PR #560 delivered the registry-backed dispatch relabeling and closed the issue claiming "total migration validated end-to-end", but as of `main` v0.285.0:

- `src/solver_handlers/` still contains **36 specialized handler files**;
- `src/solver_dispatch.rs` still routes **82 `try_*` entries**;
- issue #433's audit named ~10 fixed-enumeration handlers (opinion_question, number_riddle, algorithm, source_refresh, …) that were never generalized;
- the "algorithm that builds algorithms" meta-builder (R7, tracked as the next step in `docs/case-studies/issue-412/README.md`) still has no implementation — `write_program` remains template/curated-snippet based for the verified catalogue.

Open issue #663 (E44) migrates only the *precedence table* into seed data. This issue owns the rest of the #559 mandate: the handlers themselves.

## Requirements

1. Produce a migration ledger `data/meta/handler-migration-ledger.lino` listing every current specialized handler with its status: `migrated` (now expressed as data-driven meta-methods / substitution rules / compiled skills over the links network), `justified-native` (kept as a compiled Rust primitive with a written justification — e.g. arithmetic evaluation, I/O), or `pending`.
2. Migrate handlers in batches, generality-first: each migrated handler must be replaced by a mechanism that also covers phrasing/topic variations the old handler missed (anti-memoization: held-out paraphrase tests per batch, per the standing rule from issues #412/#433/#465).
3. The meta-builder slice: `write_program` synthesis must derive programs for tasks/languages outside the curated catalogue by composing knowledge-oracle facts + generated tests, or fail honestly with a named gap (`skill_gap`, consistent with #674).
4. The number of specialized handler files and `try_*` dispatch entries must **monotonically decrease** per batch, enforced by a ratchet test (like the file-size ratchet).
5. Behavior preservation per batch is pinned by the existing benchmark/parity fixtures; every removed handler's covered prompts keep answering identically or better.
6. Final state: dispatch consults only (a) the links store (memory), (b) the method registry driven by seed data, (c) the small justified-native primitive set. No intent-specific Rust routing remains.

## Acceptance criteria

- Ratchet test `handler_migration_ratchet` asserts `specialized_handler_count <= recorded_max` and the recorded max shrinks in every migration PR.
- `MethodRegistry` resolves every route with zero references into `SPECIALIZED_HANDLERS`-style Rust tables (completes #663 and retires the constant entirely).
- A held-out paraphrase suite per migrated batch passes, proving generalization rather than translation of hardcoding into data-hardcoding.
- `REQUIREMENTS.md` row for #559 is updated from "Implemented" to an honest per-slice status, and `ROADMAP.md` tracks this as a requirement-level status row.

## Dependencies

- Blocked by #663 (E44 precedence-to-data must land first) and #674 (E55 arbitrary NL skill compilation provides the replacement mechanism for procedural handlers).
- Blocks #657 (E38: self-authored releases are only meaningful when behavior lives in data the system can edit) and the self-coding chain #673 → #656 → #657.
- Related: #656 (E37 gates each migration batch), #662 (E43).

## Process

Collect data to `docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` with the full handler census, per-handler migration plan, and online research (production rule systems, RETE, term rewriting); single PR per batch; the issue is closed only when the final-state criterion (6) is met.
title: E57: Complete the issue-559 mandate — migrate all specialized handlers into data-driven meta-methods (only memory + meta algorithm)

Parent: #244.

## Problem
Code answers must cover the top-10 popular languages, declare their isolation level, include execution links, ship an algorithm **with tests** (TDD), translate programs between languages preserving semantics, and report execution failures with a full trace.

## Approach
Treat code generation as a loop sub-task: formalize the requested algorithm/language, generate code + at least one test (step 6 TDD), attempt execution where the surface allows it (link to E11 isolation), and emit `trace:execution_failure` on failure instead of silently passing.

## Existing components
`src/solver_helpers.rs` (`build_sorting_algorithm_answer`, hello-world seeds), `src/solver_handlers/` software-project handlers, the seed hello-world data.

## Acceptance criteria — graduate all 6 in `code_generation.rs`
`top_ten_popular_languages_each_have_a_hello_world_seed`, `code_answers_include_execution_links_in_notation`, `code_answers_declare_isolation_level`, `sorting_algorithm_request_returns_code_and_tests`, `translating_a_program_between_languages_keeps_semantics`, `execution_failures_are_reported_with_full_trace`.

Depends on: E2; isolation level coordinates with E11.

---
Part of the #244 vision-implementation plan. Full specification (problem, approach, existing components, acceptance criteria) and the dependency graph are in `ROADMAP.md` and `docs/case-studies/issue-244/proposed-issues.md`.
title: E7: Code generation & cross-language translation

body:
Parent: #244.

## Problem
The translation pipeline runs `formalize → meaning → deformalize` (`src/translation/`), but the link-native invariants are not enforced: synonyms across languages must share one meaning id, the trace must include the intermediate meaning, language tags must be declared, and untranslatable concepts must be flagged.

## Approach
Anchor meanings on the E3 Q/P-ids; persist one meaning id per concept in the E1 store; render the target surface from per-language labels/lexemes (Abstract Wikipedia/Wikifunctions model); attach the intermediate meaning to the trace; flag terms with no anchor.

## Existing components
`src/translation/pipeline.rs`, `wiktionary.rs`, `wikidata.rs`; E3 formalization; E1 store.

## Acceptance criteria — graduate all 7 in `translation_via_links.rs`
`translation_preserves_meaning_id_across_languages`, `translation_request_returns_target_surface_form`, `synonyms_across_languages_share_meaning`, `translation_declares_source_and_target_language_tags`, `translation_trace_includes_intermediate_meaning`, `cross_language_code_translation_preserves_semantics`, `untranslatable_concepts_are_flagged`.

Depends on: E2, E3.

---
Part of the #244 vision-implementation plan. Full specification (problem, approach, existing components, acceptance criteria) and the dependency graph are in `ROADMAP.md` and `docs/case-studies/issue-244/proposed-issues.md`.

body:
## Summary

Fixes #556. Generalizes the response-language follow-up from a single
repository-lookup special case into the **whole class** of "re-answer the
previous request in another language" turns, driven entirely by the
data-grounded seed lexicon and expressed as a grounded meta-algorithm.

A bare follow-up such as *"I do not understand English, write in Russian"* is not
a new question — it asks the assistant to re-answer the previous request in a
named language. Rather than localizing one handler, the follow-up now **replays
the previous user request through the entire solver** with the target language
forced at a single detection seam, so every localizable answer family
(project lookup, capabilities, identity, concept lookup, …) re-renders in the
requested language — and reverses back to English on request.

## What changed

- **Single forced-language seam** (`src/language.rs`): a thread-local override
  consulted at the top of `language::detect`, installed by an RAII guard
  (`set_forced_language`). One seam localizes the whole solver — no per-handler
  threading. Mirrored in JS as a module-global `FORCED_RESPONSE_LANGUAGE`
  consulted at the top of `detectLanguage`.
- **Whole-solver replay** (`src/solver_handlers/response_language_followup.rs`):
  recovers the prior user request from history and replays it with the target
  language forced. Recursion-guarded (a replay already carries a forced language)
  and inconclusive-guarded (unknown/ill_formed/clarify replays fall through).
- **Seed-grounded triggers** (issue #386 convention): the requested language and
  the comprehension-failure marker are recognised by *role* from
  `data/seed/meanings-translation.lino`, grounded in Wikidata
  (`understanding` Q46744; English Q1860, Russian Q7737, Hindi Q1568,
  Chinese Q7850) — never a hardcoded phrase table.
- **Grounded meta-algorithm recipe**
  (`data/meta/response-language-followup-recipe.lino`): a machine-readable,
  eight-step description of the universal recursive-reasoning algorithm, pinned
  by `tests/unit/specification/response_language_meta_algorithm.rs` (8 grounding
  tests) so the recipe can never silently drift from the live source. Documented
  in `docs/meta-algorithm.md`.

## Testing (every finest detail, all four languages)

- `tests/unit/specification/response_language_followup.rs` — capabilities and
  identity re-render in ru/hi/zh, reverse to English on request, and retarget
  between two non-English languages (Russian context → Chinese) proving the seam
  is target-driven and language-agnostic.
- `tests/unit/specification/project_lookups.rs` — the original issue #556
  repository lookup across all four seeded languages.
- `tests/unit/specification/translation_round_trip.rs` — issue #526 round-trip:
  English↔Russian/Hindi/Chinese vocabulary survives a
  source→meta-language→target→source cycle with both meaning and surface
  preserved.
- `tests/unit/specification/response_language_meta_algorithm.rs` — 8 tests
  grounding the recipe against the live source (steps, roles, Wikidata cache,
  functions, seam, Rust↔JS parity, pinning tests).
- `experiments/js_worker_followup_harness.mjs` — 5/5 JS worker cases
  (project_lookup / capabilities / identity across ru/hi/zh + cross-language),
  proving Rust↔JS parity.

Full suite: **1245 unit tests pass**; `cargo fmt --check` and `cargo clippy
--all-targets` clean.

## Issue #556 requirements → evidence

| Requirement | Where |
| --- | --- |
| Generalize to the whole class in all languages | whole-solver replay + `response_language_followup.rs` (4 langs) |
| Universal meta-algorithm, recursive steps in meta language | `response-language-followup-recipe.lino` + `response_language_meta_algorithm.rs` |
| Meanings grounded in external data sources | seed roles grounded in Wikidata Q46744/Q1860/Q7737/Q1568/Q7850 |
| Full meta-language translation cycle | `translate_via_default_pipeline` round-trips through the meta language |
| Issue #526 round-trip testing | `translation_round_trip.rs` |
| Every finest detail tested / JS parity | 1245 unit tests + 5/5 JS harness cases |

## Summary

Resolves the umbrella translation issue #218 and the follow-up PR feedback by fixing the reported apple translation bugs and adding supported-language coverage guards so future feature fixes cannot silently cover only English/Russian.

- Fixes #216 — `translate apple to russian` now returns `яблоко` instead of `[ru]`.
- Fixes #217 — `переведи «яблоко» на английский` now returns `apple` instead of `"[en] яблоко"`.
- Extends unquoted translation prompts across all supported languages: English, Russian, Hindi, and Chinese.
- Adds CI coverage checks for tool localization, feature-capability test matrices, concept-context labels, and browser translation registry entries across every supported language.

## What changed

### Rust core
- `src/translation/prompt.rs`: extracts unquoted translation surfaces from English, Russian, Hindi, and Chinese prompt forms.
- `src/solver_helpers.rs`: detects native source/target language markers and infers source language from the quoted/unquoted surface before falling back to instruction language.
- `src/solver_handlers/mod.rs`: keeps Hindi/Chinese `translate` capability questions out of the translation handler unless a target language is present.
- `examples/refresh_translation_cache.rs` and `data/translation-cache/`: seed single-noun `apple` targets for Russian, Hindi, and Chinese.

### Browser worker
- Mirrors the Rust prompt extraction and source/target detection in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`.
- Extends `TRANSLATION_MEANING_REGISTRY` so browser fallback meanings cover English, Russian, Hindi, and Chinese.

### Tests and CI guards
- `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs`: covers unquoted `apple` translation into every supported target plus native Hindi/Chinese prompt forms.
- `tests/e2e/tests/issue-218.spec.js`: browser regressions cover English/Russian/Hindi/Chinese targets and native Hindi/Chinese prompts.
- `tests/unit/specification/capabilities.rs`: every feature capability is tested in every supported language.
- `tests/e2e/scripts/check-multilingual-intent-coverage.mjs`: CI now verifies supported-language coverage for localized responses, concepts, concept contexts, tools, feature capabilities, prompt-pattern groups, and browser translation meanings.

### Case study
- `docs/case-studies/issue-218/` documents the timeline, root causes, online references, before/after behavior, and verification evidence.

## Before / after

| Prompt | Before | After |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `translate apple to russian` | `[ru]` | `яблоко` |
| `Translate Apple to Russian` | `[ru]` | `Яблоко` |
| `переведи «яблоко» на английский` | `[en] яблоко` | `apple` |
| `translate apple to hindi` | `[hi]` / fallback | `सेब` |
| `translate apple to chinese` | `[zh]` / fallback | `蘋果` / `苹果` |
| `apple का हिंदी में अनुवाद करो` | unknown fallback | `सेब` |
| `把 apple 翻译成中文` | unknown fallback | `蘋果` / `苹果` |

## Test plan

- [x] `rust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs`
- [x] `cargo fmt --all -- --check`

## Upstream reports

None filed. The defects were local to this repository: Wiktionary serves the needed translation tables, Wikidata lexemes are linked, and the missing pieces were local cache coverage, prompt extraction, browser fallback data, and multilingual CI guards.

body:
## Summary
- Replace the robotic `meaning: …\nsurface (…): …` translation body with the deformalized target surface (still quoted when the user quoted the source), so the assistant reads like natural conversation.
- Resolve any surface pair through a real `formalize → meaning → deformalize` pipeline backed by Wiktionary translation tables and Wikidata sense joins — not a hand-written phrase list. The pipeline lives under `src/translation/` (`http`, `cache`, `wiktionary`, `wikidata`, `meaning`, `pipeline`, `formatting`).
- Cache raw HTTP responses under `data/translation-cache/<fnv1a>.body` + `.url` via `CachedHttpClient` so unit tests run deterministically offline against the committed cache; live mode is gated by `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API` and can be refreshed with `examples/refresh_translation_cache.rs`.
- Add `match_source_formatting` / `matchSourceFormatting` helpers so the source fragment's leading-letter case and terminal punctuation survive the round-trip (`как у тебя дела?` → `how are you?`, `Как у тебя дела` → `How are you`).
- Document the change with R213/R214/R215 in `REQUIREMENTS.md`, the pipeline + browser fallback in `ARCHITECTURE.md` §10, the surface-preservation invariant in `VISION.md`, and a full case study under `docs/case-studies/issue-207/`.

Fixes #207.

## Reproduction
Before: the prompt `Переведи "как у тебя дела?" на английский.` produced a robotic
```
meaning: meaning_2cfc55c914d57d9e
surface (ru): как у тебя дела?
surface (en): How are you?
```
After: the assistant answers
```
"how are you?"
```
with the meaning id + source/target slugs preserved in the Links Notation trace (`evidence_links`) rather than in the human-readable body. Lowercase source → lowercase target; capitalized source → capitalized target. The pipeline no longer relies on a hand-written list: any surface routed through `TranslationPipeline::translate` resolves via Wiktionary + Wikidata.

## Architecture
The pipeline (`src/translation/pipeline.rs`):
1. Fetches the source-edition Wiktionary page and parses its `{{trans-top}}` blocks for `target_lang` candidates.
2. Falls back to the `/translations` subpage when the main page omits translations.
3. Falls back to the target-edition Wiktionary page in reverse when the source edition is sparse (typical for ru → en).
4. Generates phrasal variants (e.g. dropping Russian "у тебя", "у вас", "у меня" infixes) when the literal page does not exist.
5. Selects the best sense block by round-trip confirmation rate.
6. Upgrades the meaning id to a Wikidata Q-item / sense id via the canonical `ontolex:sense / wdt:P5137` lexeme join.

The Rust pipeline is the canonical implementation. The browser worker (`src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`) cannot reach Wiktionary or Wikidata directly because of browser CORS restrictions, so it keeps a small offline phrase table as a CORS-safe fallback for the GitHub Pages demo.

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
- [x] `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- [x] `cargo test` (406 unit tests passing, 69 ignored as tracked future work)
- [x] New unit tests in `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs` cover (a) lowercase preservation, (b) terminal-punctuation preservation when absent in source, (c) eight unrelated phrase pairs across en/ru/hi/zh routed entirely through cached real-world responses.
- [x] `tests/unit/docs_requirements.rs::issue_207_natural_translation_documents_are_present_and_traceable` ensures REQUIREMENTS.md / ARCHITECTURE.md / VISION.md / case-study stay in sync with the new architecture.

## Notes for reviewers
- The first attempt at a "shared offline meaning registry" was rejected by the maintainer as a fake solution. The new implementation removes that registry entirely and routes every translation through Wiktionary's translation tables and Wikidata's SPARQL lexeme join.
- Hash invariants for already-issued ids are preserved: surfaces still hash through the same `stable_id` scheme; the Wikidata Q-id upgrade happens at the `MeaningId` layer when the lexeme join succeeds.
- Cached responses are committed (206 files under `data/translation-cache/`) so CI is offline and deterministic; contributors can refresh them with `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1 cargo run --example refresh_translation_cache`.
title: feat: generalized translation via Wiktionary + Wikidata pipeline

- **Locale**: en-US (Asia/Calcutta)

## Reproduction of dialog

Legend: `U` = user, `A` = agent.

```
U: What is the most popular dataset for translation quality validation?
A (intent: unknown, reported): That one is new to me. I cannot answer that from local Links Notation rules yet. To inspect what I can do, send `List behavior rules`, then `Show behavior rule unknown`. To teach this dialog a response, send: When I say `your prompt`, answer `your answer`. To make it durable, export memory or use Report issue so developers can add the fact or rule to the seed.
```

## Description

We need to find a way to be able to answer all such questions in general, not just this one question.

That is the answer from Google Gemini:

```
The most popular standard for translation quality validation is the WMT Test Sets (e.g., $\text{newstest}$ series) used in the annual WMT Conference on Machine Translation. These gold-standard benchmarks include multi-domain human-written references and fine-grained Multidimensional Quality Metrics (MQM) annotations. [[1](https://custom.mt/top-100-open-datasets-to-train-ai-translation-models/), [2](https://www2.statmt.org/wmt26/translation-task.html), [3](https://aclanthology.org/2022.wmt-1.3/), [4](https://www.smartling.com/blog/how-to-assess-machine-translation-quality#:~:text=The%20first%20is%20monthly%2C%20third%2Dparty%20multidimensional%20quality,severity%20of%20errors%20found%20in%20translated%20text.)]  
For massive multilingual and low-resource evaluation, the FLORES-200 dataset (developed by Meta AI) serves as the primary modern alternative. [[5](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/flores-200-benchmark-dataset#:~:text=The%20FLORES%2D200%20Benchmark%20Dataset%20is%20a%20large%2Dscale%2C,development%20for%20low%2Dresource%20and%20typologically%20diverse%20languages.), [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2401.16313v1#:~:text=FLORES%2D101%20(Goyal%20et%20al.%2C%202022)%20and%20FLORES%2D200,data%20in%20101%20and%20200%20languages%20respectively.), [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.04797v1#:~:text=FLORES%2D200%20represents%20one%20of%20the%20largest%20human%2Dannotated,across%20a%20wide%20range%20of%20linguistic%20families.)]  
If you want to tailor validation for your use case, let me know: 

• What is the target domain/industry? (e.g., technical, medical, legal, e-commerce) 
• Are you looking to evaluate a specific language pair (e.g., English $\to$ German) or a global model? 
• What metrics do you currently use? (e.g., COMET, BLEU, human-in-the-loop evaluation) 

I can recommend the most fitting benchmark or dataset size for your workflow. 

AI responses may include mistakes.

[1] https://custom.mt/top-100-open-datasets-to-train-ai-translation-models/

There isn’t a single universally dominant dataset for translation quality validation, because the field uses a combination of:

1. **Benchmark corpora** (for testing translations)
2. **Human evaluation frameworks**
3. **Automatic evaluation metrics**

But in practice, these are the closest things to “the most popular” standards:

---

## 1. WMT datasets (Workshop on Machine Translation) — the de facto benchmark

The most widely used benchmark suite in machine translation research is the [Workshop on Machine Translation](chatgpt://generic-entity?number=0) shared-task datasets. Nearly every major MT paper reports results on WMT test sets.  [oai_citation:0‡MIT Press Direct](https://direct.mit.edu/tacl/article/doi/10.1162/tacl_a_00683/124263/xcomet-Transparent-Machine-Translation-Evaluation?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Examples:
- WMT14 En↔De
- WMT17
- WMT20
- WMT24

These datasets are used to:
- compare MT systems,
- validate translation quality metrics,
- train/evaluate quality estimation systems.

---

## 2. MQM annotations — the current “gold standard” for human validation

For **human translation quality validation**, the most influential framework today is:

## MQM (Multidimensional Quality Metrics)

MQM is not just a dataset — it’s a structured annotation framework where expert annotators label:
- accuracy errors,
- fluency errors,
- terminology issues,
- severity (minor/major/critical),
- exact spans of errors.

It is widely considered the modern gold standard for evaluating translation quality.  [oai_citation:1‡Google Research](https://research.google/pubs/experts-errors-and-context-a-large-scale-study-of-human-evaluation-for-machine-translation/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

The most cited MQM-based evaluation datasets come from:
- WMT 2020–2024 MQM evaluations,
- Google MQM studies,
- Unbabel/COMET training corpora.

The landmark paper:
[Experts, Errors, and Context: A Large-Scale Study of Human Evaluation for Machine Translation](chatgpt://generic-entity?number=1)
helped establish MQM as the preferred expert evaluation methodology.  [oai_citation:2‡Google Research](https://research.google/pubs/experts-errors-and-context-a-large-scale-study-of-human-evaluation-for-machine-translation/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## 3. FLORES-200 — most popular multilingual benchmark

For multilingual translation benchmarking, the most widely adopted recent dataset is:

[FLORES-200](chatgpt://generic-entity?number=2)

It covers:
- 200+ languages,
- low-resource languages,
- standardized evaluation.

Modern LLM translation papers frequently benchmark on FLORES alongside WMT.  [oai_citation:3‡Awesome Agents](https://awesomeagents.ai/leaderboards/translation-benchmarks-leaderboard/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## 4. COMET — most popular automatic quality metric today

If by “validation” you mean *automatic scoring*, then:

[COMET](chatgpt://generic-entity?number=3)
is currently the most popular high-performing evaluation metric. It correlates better with human judgments than classic BLEU.  [oai_citation:4‡help.inten.to](https://help.inten.to/article/mt-studio-mt-quality-metrics?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Historically:
- BLEU was the standard,
- now COMET and MetricX dominate research leaderboards.

---

## Short answer

| Purpose | Most popular |
|---|---|
| General MT benchmarking | WMT datasets |
| Human quality validation | MQM annotations |
| Multilingual benchmarking | FLORES-200 |
| Automatic scoring metric | COMET |
| Historical metric | BLEU |

So if someone says:
> “What dataset/framework is the industry standard for translation quality validation?”

the safest answer today is:

> **WMT + MQM annotations**, often evaluated with **COMET**.
```

We can merge multiple translation of definitions from different languages in Wikipedia of the same term, to have the richest definition by combination. That will always be better, than just using wikipedia directly.

Also it can help us to learn the translation rules. As article definitions in different languages should be similar and quality of their translation should be high.

We need to solve general case, not specifics. We need to be able to learn how to formalize, translate formalized content to another language, make tens and hundreds of tests. The translation should be stable, and we should be able to find a way to translate in to ways, in such a way, what if we take our translation and translate it back, we will get no data loss (ideally), if 100% is not possible, we should try to do it at 99% and so on.

No neural networks, no learnings, just algorithms and reasoning, data from the wikipedia, internet and so on.
title: Be better than wikipedia, by merging multiple translations of definitions

body:
## Summary

Fixes #221 — stops faking translation for common nouns. The Rust
pipeline and the browser demo now translate any reachable noun through
the real `source → formalize → meaning → deformalize → target` path
using only Wikipedia / Wikidata / Wiktionary data. No LLM in the loop,
no pre-extracted dictionary in the repo.

**This revision (2026-05-22) follows the PR review feedback:** the
previous 128-entry offline dictionary is gone. The only legal cached
artefact is now a **verbatim API response body** stored as
**human-readable raw text** inside indented Links Notation files under
`data/seed/api-cache/`, capped at 128 records per semantic bucket and
1500 lines per file. Bodies use Links Notation's doubled-quote escape
(one literal `"` is written as two consecutive `"`); no other character
is escaped, so reviewers can read the raw API JSON directly. Anything
that cannot be served from the bundle falls through to the on-disk
accelerator (gitignored) and then to live HTTP. The browser worker has
its own live fallback so it never needs a bundled dictionary.

Full investigation lives in `docs/case-studies/issue-221/`
(timeline through 2026-05-22, eleven verbatim requirements, five root
causes, fixes, before/after table, verification commands, upstream
defect notes, and online research on Wiktionary CORS / Wikidata SPARQL
polysemy).

## Changes

### Raw API-response seed bundle (replaces the dictionary)
- `data/seed/api-cache/*.lino` — verbatim Wikidata and Wiktionary API
  response bodies, indented Links Notation with **plain-text** payloads
  (chunks split at ≤200 chars; values use Links Notation's
  doubled-quote escape — one literal `"` becomes `""`). Bundle is
  capped at 128 records per bucket (entities, properties, search,
  sparql, pages-per-language) and every file stays under 1500 lines;
  oversize bodies split into `<bucket>-partN.lino` parts that re-join
  at load by URL.
- `build.rs` enumerates the `.lino` files at compile time and emits
  `seed_bundle_files.rs` into `OUT_DIR`; `src/translation/cache.rs`
  picks it up via `include!(concat!(env!("OUT_DIR"), ...))` so adding
  a new part file is automatic — no per-file `include_str!` edit.
- `examples/refresh_translation_cache.rs` drives the full pipeline
  against a curated 128-noun seed list, populates the on-disk
  accelerator, then rebundles into the committed `.lino` files
  (splitting oversize records, cleaning stale parts).
- `experiments/convert_base64_seed_to_text.rs` — one-shot migration
  tool used to rewrite the historical base64-chunked bundle into the
  new plain-text layout. Kept for reproducibility.
- **Deleted**: `data/seed/translations.lino`,
  `src/translation/dictionary.rs`, `examples/build_translation_dictionary.rs`.

### Semantic-identity HTTP cache
- `src/translation/cache.rs` keys URLs by **kind of data**, not URL
  hash: `data/wikidata-cache/{search,entities,query,sparql}/`,
  `data/wiktionary-cache/<lang>/`, `data/http-cache/misc/`. All three
  roots are gitignored — the on-disk cache is a local accelerator
  written by `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1` runs; the committed source of
  truth is the seed bundle above.
- `CachedHttpClient::get` consults three layers in order: seed bundle
  → on-disk accelerator → live HTTP.

### Rust pipeline
- `src/translation/wikidata.rs` — SPARQL lexeme join now requires
  `wikibase:lexicalCategory` to match between source and target
  lexemes, so polysemous surfaces like `water` no longer cross
  noun ↔ verb boundaries (was `water → поливать`, now `water → вода`).
- `src/translation/pipeline.rs` — Stage 1a follows
  `{{see translation subpage|...}}` to the `/translations` subpage
  when the main page only carries the marker.
  `FORMAL_AI_TRANSLATION_DEBUG=1` enables verbose stage-by-stage
  tracing (closes the issue #218 future-work item).

### Browser worker
- `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js` — removed `TRANSLATION_DICTIONARY` and
  `lookupDictionary`. Added `liveWiktionaryTranslate(surface, source,
  target)` that calls
  `*.wiktionary.org/w/api.php?action=parse&page=...&prop=wikitext&format=json&origin=*`
  directly, follows `{{see translation subpage|...}}` to the
  `/translations` subpage, and extracts the `{{tt+|<lang>|...}}` /
  `{{t|...}}` template payload. `translateSurface` and `tryTranslation`
  are now async; the only caller already runs inside `async`.
- `src/web/seed_loader.js` — removed the `extractTranslations` parser
  and the `translations` field; the worker no longer hydrates an
  offline dictionary.
- Mobile-friendly by design: nothing pre-bundled, the MediaWiki action
  API is CORS-friendly through `origin=*`, the seed bundle keeps the
  worker payload tiny.

### Tests & docs
- `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs` — `issue_221_*`
  regressions covering quoted + unquoted common-noun translation in
  both directions.
- `tests/unit/data_files.rs` — verifies every shipped `.lino` file
  parses through `lino_objects_codec::format::parse_indented`, stays
  under 1500 lines, and never falls back to typed-object encoding.
- `tests/unit/docs_requirements.rs` — adds `data/seed/api-cache` to
  the `is_skipped_tree` allowlist so the deferred-labels scan does
  not trip on verbatim Wikipedia wikitext that legitimately contains
  forbidden substrings (`mvp`, `poc`, etc.).
- `tests/e2e/tests/issue-221.spec.js` — Playwright coverage of the
  live-worker path: quoted RU→EN, quoted EN→RU, unquoted prompts,
  Russian inflected forms via MediaWiki redirect, round-trip stability.
- `docs/case-studies/issue-221/{README.md,online-research.md}` —
  rewritten for the API-cache + live-worker architecture; preserves
  the original investigation timeline and adds the 2026-05-22 entries.
- `changelog.d/20260522_000500_issue_221_translation_common_nouns.md`
  — rewritten changelog fragment.

## Before / After

| Prompt | Before (v0.100.0) | After |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `Переведи "помидор" на английский.` | `"[en] помидор"` | `"tomato"` |
| `Переведи "огурец" на английский.` | `"[en] огурец"` | `"cucumber"` |
| `переведи "картофель" на английский` | `"[en] картофель"` | `"potato"` |
| `translate "tomato" to russian` | `"[ru] tomato"` | `"помидор"` |
| `translate "carrot" to russian` | `"[ru] carrot"` | `"морковь"` |
| `translate "water" to russian` | `"поливать"` (verb!) | `"вода"` |
| `переведи помидор на английский` (unquoted) | `"[en] помидор"` | `"tomato"` |

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — clean.
- [x] `cargo clippy --release --all-targets -- -D warnings` — clean.
- [x] `cargo fmt --check` — clean.
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 229 lib + 11 integration + 462 unit
  tests pass, including the `issue_221_*` regressions, the existing
  issue #218 coverage, and the new `lino_data_files_are_parseable_…`
  parser-roundtrip check on the regenerated seed bundle.
- [x] `rust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs` — all shipped files
  inside their configured caps.
- [x] `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1 cargo run --release --example refresh_translation_cache`
  — every seeded entry resolved; bundle re-written under
  `data/seed/api-cache/` within the 128-record / 1500-line caps.
- [ ] CI green on `windows-latest`, `macos-latest`, `ubuntu-latest`,
  plus Lint, Format, Coverage, and E2E (waiting on this push).
- [ ] `npm --prefix tests/e2e run test:local -- tests/issue-221.spec.js`
  — exercises the live Wiktionary fallback in the browser worker
  (requires network access during the run).

Fixes #221


## CI follow-ups (2026-05-22, after re-run)

Four small fixes layered on top of the seed-bundle rewrite so the
pipeline survives the live-API-first browser path on CI's network and
honours the reviewer's "must be human-readable" requirement:

- **`build.rs` — preserve chunk order across split records.** The
  lexicographic `files.sort()` placed `wiktionary-pages-part10.lino`
  before `-part2.lino` and the bare `wiktionary-pages.lino` (chunk 0)
  after every part, so any record split into multiple `.lino` files
  had its chunks reassembled out of order. Sort by
  `(bucket, part_index)` instead. Only split records were affected
  (apple failed; tomato fit in a single chunk).
- **`extractWiktionaryTranslation` — handle Russian Wiktionary
  blocks.** Russian Wiktionary uses
  `{{перев-блок|<sense>\n|<lang>=[[surface]]\n|...}}` rather than the
  English `{{t|<lang>|...}}` template family. The worker now matches
  both. Also added an NFD-based combining-mark strip so English
  Wiktionary's `{{t+|ru|помидо́р}}` (with U+0301 acute) resolves to the
  expected `помидор`.
- **`liveWiktionaryTranslate` — parallelize direct fetch and lemma
  resolution.** Inflected forms like `помидоры` need a 404, an
  OpenSearch round-trip to the lemma `помидор`, and a fetch of that
  lemma page. Running the first fetch and the OpenSearch lookup via
  `Promise.all` saves one sequential round-trip and keeps the assertion
  under Playwright's 5 s `expect` cap in CI.
- **`data/seed/api-cache/*.lino` — store responses as plain text.**
  Reviewer feedback: "We should not encode raw API data in .lino files
  as base64, it should be all human readable." Cache reader/writer
  now use Links Notation's doubled-quote escape (`""` represents one
  literal `"`) instead of base64; chunker never starts a chunk with
  `"` so split points always land on the safe side of an LN delimiter
  run. Wiktionary bucket shrinks from 18 to 4 parts (raw text is ~⅓
  the size of its base64 form).
title: Replace pre-seeded dictionary with raw API-response cache (issue #221)

**Foundation.** Parent: #244.

## Problem
Formalization is alias-based (`src/concepts.rs`). The vision needs arbitrary prompts mapped to language-independent meanings anchored on Wikidata **P-ids** (verbs/properties) and **Q-ids** (nouns/items), with Wiktionary/Wikipedia fallbacks (`ARCHITECTURE.md` §5, §16.1).

## Approach
1. Build a multilingual labels table (cached via E5) and per-language morphology hints to resolve surface tokens to candidate Q/P-ids.
2. Emit formalization candidates as scored meaning records (consumed by E4's selection) with source links to the anchor.
3. Fall back to Wiktionary/Wikipedia surfaces when no Q/P-id exists; flag terms with no anchor as untranslatable inputs (feeds E6).

## Existing components
Wikidata SPARQL (`src/translation/wikidata.rs`), Wiktionary parser (`src/translation/wiktionary.rs`), `src/concepts.rs` aliases as the seed; Abstract Wikipedia/Wikifunctions prior art.

## Acceptance criteria
Closes `ARCHITECTURE.md` §16.1. Add tests covering P/Q-id extraction over arbitrary prompts (verb→P-id, noun→Q-id, fallback to surface, unknown→flagged). Enables E6 meaning-id tests and the E10 type-chain test.

Depends on: E2.

---
Part of the #244 vision-implementation plan. Full specification (problem, approach, existing components, acceptance criteria) and the dependency graph are in `ROADMAP.md` and `docs/case-studies/issue-244/proposed-issues.md`.
title: E3: Full Wikidata P/Q-id formalization engine

body:
## E22 — Intent formalization as Links Notation (drop the fixed intent catalogue)

Part of the issue #244 vision batch (E21-E27).

### Problem (current code)

Routing is driven by a **fixed catalogue of intents**, not by formalizing the
message:

- `src/engine.rs` — `select_rule_for()` (`src/engine.rs:670`) maps prompts onto the closed `SelectedRule` enum (`src/engine.rs:618`).
- `src/solver.rs` — `SPECIALIZED_HANDLERS` (`src/solver.rs:418`) is an ordered table of ~35 keyword/phrase handlers; the first to match claims the impulse.
- A Wikidata-backed `FormalizationCandidate` representation exists in `src/translation/formalization.rs` but is **not** the primary routing input.

VISION.md asks the opposite: "formalize the messages of the user … intent as
Links Notation or binary-links encoded semantic meta-language", and "record all
our previous reasoning about previous messages, to help us save time on
re-reasoning at each new message".

### Goal

Every incoming message is first **formalized into a Links-Notation / binary-links
intent** — a link structure capturing kind (task / question / requirement),
what is known, and what is relevant — before any routing decision. Routing then
matches against the formalized intent, not a hardcoded enum. Prior formalizations
and their reasoning are **cached** keyed by content-addressed impulse id so a
repeated or near-identical message is not re-reasoned from scratch.

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A formalization stage converts an arbitrary prompt into a `.lino` intent structure (kind, knowns, relevants) recorded in the event log / durable store.
- [ ] Routing consumes the formalized intent; the closed `SelectedRule` enum / `SPECIALIZED_HANDLERS` table is no longer the first-class router (handlers become resolvable *from* the formalized intent).
- [ ] Repeated messages hit a reasoning cache keyed by impulse id; the trace shows a cache hit instead of re-formalization.
- [ ] `src/translation/formalization.rs` `FormalizationCandidate` is wired into the routing path (or explicitly superseded by the new intent structure).
- [ ] New specification tests: prompt → intent links, cache-hit on repeat, routing decision derived from the intent links.

### Existing components to reuse

- `src/translation/formalization.rs` (`FormalizationCandidate`, `FormalizationSlot`, `FormalizationAnchor`) and Wikidata P/Q anchoring (E3).
- `src/concepts.rs` alias resolution as a fallback anchor source.
- `src/memory.rs` / `.lino` durable store for the reasoning cache.
- Links Notation tooling (`lino-objects-codec`, `lino-arguments`).

### Prior art

Abstract Wikipedia / Wikifunctions (language-independent meaning anchored on
Wikidata); OpenCog AtomSpace rule-as-data.
title: E22: Intent formalization as Links Notation (drop the fixed intent catalogue)

body:
## Summary

Fixes #676.

Many commands and prompts failed when Formal AI was reached through an agentic CLI (OpenCode). Every failure traced back to the same class of root cause on the **Formal AI side**: a slightly different phrasing than a seed anticipated fell through to the *unknown* fallback, and the reasoning trace shown to the client was an identical robotic category template. OpenCode itself behaves correctly — it forwards the prompt and renders whatever the server returns (see [`upstream.md`](docs/case-studies/issue-676/upstream.md)).

Each fix is applied to **both** the Rust engine and the JS web worker mirror, and is covered by unit + e2e tests.

## Requirements addressed

The issue's asks were extracted into stable IDs in the [case study](docs/case-studies/issue-676/README.md):

| ID | Requirement | Status |
|----|-------------|--------|
| **R1** | `execute pwd` (and other shell tokens) in agentic mode | ✅ Fixed |
| **R2** | Natural-language file listing (`give me a list of files…`) | ✅ Fixed |
| **R3** | Naming (`Now your name is Ineffa`) + later recall | ✅ Fixed |
| **R4** | `How are you?` should not return the generic greeting | ✅ Fixed |
| **R5** | Self-healing on natural self-directed repair requests | ✅ Fixed |
| **R6** | Support as many message variations as possible | ✅ Broadened + tested |
| **R7** | Auto-learning / meta self-solve | ✅ Verified (self-heal + learning loop) |
| **R8** | Higher-quality, human-like thinking display | ✅ Fixed (primary focus) |
| **R9** | Deep case study in `./docs/case-studies/issue-676` | ✅ Delivered |
| **R10** | Debug/verbose tracing where root cause wasn't obvious | ✅ Added |
| **R11** | Report upstream issues if caused by another project | ✅ Triaged — no upstream bug |
| **R12** | Apply fixes across the whole codebase (Rust + JS) | ✅ Parity enforced by CI |
| **R13** | Land everything in this single PR | ✅ |

## What changed

### Agentic terminal commands (R1, R2)
`src/agentic_coding/planner.rs` now runs **any** seed shell token (`pwd`, `git`, `cargo`, `whoami`, …) named in a prompt, not just `ls`. `execute pwd`, `run git status`, and their many phrasings map to the real command. Natural-language file-listing requests resolve to `ls` across far more phrasings.

### Naming + recall (R3)
`src/solver_handlers/conversation_memory/` honours being named in conversation ("Now your name is Ineffa", "I'll call you Ada", "you are called …") and recalls it when later asked "what is your name", using dialog-local memory with **no server state** — mirrored in the browser worker.

### `How are you?` small talk (R4)
A dedicated `wellbeing` intent is matched **before** `greeting` (first-match-wins), so "how are you", "как дела", "आप कैसे हैं", and "你好吗" get an actual warm answer across en/ru/hi/zh instead of the generic greeting.

### Self-healing (R5, R7)
`src/agentic_coding/self_heal.rs` now triggers on natural self-directed repair requests ("Can you fix it yourself?", "debug yourself", "heal yourself") while ordinary "fix this file" requests stay out of the repair loop. Verified against the existing self-healing + learning-ledger loop.

### Human thinking display (R8 — primary focus)
The reasoning trace used to be a fixed six-line template ("Read the request … Detect the request language …") that differed between unrelated prompts only by a route label buried mid-list — which is exactly what OpenCode surfaced as a robotic "Thought" block.

Now every trace **opens with a per-intent, first-person narrative** of what the assistant understood and decided ("You asked how I'm doing, so I told you and offered to help."), with the concrete steps preserved beneath it as an expandable, **recursive "robotic detail"** layer. Applied to:
- the API/CLI `reasoning` field that agentic clients render (`src/thinking.rs` `thinking_narrative` → `render_thinking_steps`);
- the web thinking preview (`src/web/app/main.jsx` `ThinkingPreview`) across **en/ru/zh/hi**, without breaking issue-488's exact step counts (a separately-testid'd headline element).

### Message-variation coverage (R6)
Each fix ships a broad set of phrasings plus unit tests asserting many variants (`data/seed/greetings.lino`, `intent-routing.lino`, `prompt-patterns.lino`, `multilingual-responses.lino`).

### Code organization (keep the 1000-line CI gate green)
The R1/R2 growth pushed two files over the repository's 1000-line hard limit, so the self-contained clusters were extracted into sibling submodules — pure code movement, public paths unchanged:
- `src/agentic_coding/shell_command.rs` — the data-driven shell resolution split out of `planner.rs` (1142 → 899 lines).
- `src/solver_helpers/code.rs` — the code/program-translation cluster split out of `solver_helpers` (1042 → 719 lines); `solver_helpers.rs` becomes a directory module re-exporting the submodule so `crate::solver_helpers::*` paths stay stable.

## Tests

- **Rust**: full `cargo test` suite green — **2094 passed, 0 failed, 2 ignored** across all binaries (count reflects the merge of the latest `main`). New: `tests/unit/issue_676_naming.rs`, `tests/unit/issue_676_thinking_narrative.rs`, plus additions to `agentic_coding.rs`, `formal_ai.rs`, `multilingual_variations.rs`. Regenerated the self-AST / self-healing census golden fixtures after the planner source change (formatting + module extraction) so they match a fresh parse.
- **e2e**: `tests/e2e/tests/issue-676-thinking-narrative.spec.js` (3 tests) — narrative headline leads the trace and robotic detail survives on expand; greeting vs wellbeing get distinct headlines; calculation route reads as a calculation. Registered in `playwright.local.config.js`.
- **Gates**: `cargo fmt --check` clean, `cargo clippy --all-targets` 0 warnings, total-reference-closure `unresolved: {}`, i18n catalog check passes (4 locales, 409 keys), multilingual intent-coverage parity green.

## Case study & upstream triage (R9, R11)

Full root-cause analysis, evidence images, and verification strategy live in [`docs/case-studies/issue-676/`](docs/case-studies/issue-676/). Upstream triage in [`upstream.md`](docs/case-studies/issue-676/upstream.md) concludes the defects are Formal-AI-side; OpenCode forwards and renders correctly, so no upstream report is warranted.
title: Fix commands/prompts failing via OpenCode: agentic shell, naming, wellbeing, self-heal, human thinking trace (issue #676)

## Reproduction of dialog

```
U: Переведи "как у тебя дела?" на английский.
A (intent: translate_ru_to_en, reported): meaning: meaning_2cfc55c914d57d9e
   surface (ru): как у тебя дела?
   surface (en): How are you?
```

## Description

The output is too robotic, we need to make it feel like natural conversation. Also we should prefer the original formatting of the translated message as well. For example if user started question from lower case latter, we should also start the translation with lower case later. So we should assume the flow, that with lowest possible probability leads for the user to introduce corrections.

We need to make sure to update our requirements and docs, to make sure in the future we will follow these guidelines. And implement fix for that case and add ability to translate not only single hardcoded `meaning_2cfc55c914d57d9e `, but all of them, meaning we need to have general logic for translation assisted by wikipedia/wikidata/wiktionary API.

So we should use formalize from source language to semantic meta language (in links, links notation), and after that just deformalize it to the target language. That should actually produce the most efficient flow of translation, as everything goes through single semantic meta language.

We should apply it all to all related places in the codebase and docs.

We need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).

Please plan and execute everything in a single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally done.

body:
## Summary

Tool/intent routing is **phrasing-gated across every tool**, not intent-based — contrary to the project's vision of *data-driven meanings and intent-level abstraction* (README: "Rather than hardcoding handlers per phrase … intent patterns … merged definition prompts … intent-level abstraction across linguistic variation"; the Universal Problem-Solving Algorithm is meant to be the single path for greetings, math, code, and novel prompts alike). Today, a handful of memorized phrasings route correctly and close synonyms fall to `UNKNOWN` or **misroute to the wrong tool**. This issue is the umbrella for **generalizing intent routing** so that, for every intent/action, **10–20+ natural-language variations per language** all resolve to the same tool by generalization — not by enumerating phrases.

Verified on v0.297.1 (`cargo install formal-ai`), `serve --agent-mode`, macOS arm64.

## Evidence — pass/fail across many phrasings (same advertised tools each run)

**web_fetch (concrete URL present → intent is unambiguous): 10/18**
```
OK  fetch / get the contents of / download / open / load / retrieve / show me / get / visit / browse to  <url>
XX  read <url>                    -> UNKNOWN
XX  summarize <url>               -> TEXT (no fetch)
XX  what does <url> say?          -> web_search      (misroute)
XX  check <url>                   -> UNKNOWN
XX  grab <url>                    -> UNKNOWN
XX  pull the page <url>           -> UNKNOWN
XX  tell me about <url>           -> web_search       (misroute, also mangles URL)
XX  what is on <url>              -> UNKNOWN
```

**read_file: 9/15** — read intent scatters across the WRONG tools:
```
OK  read / read the file / show me the contents of / open / print / show / get the contents of / read file / show file
XX  cat 1.txt              -> exec_command
XX  display 1.txt          -> web_fetch     (!)
XX  view the file 1.txt    -> web_search     (!)
XX  load 1.txt             -> web_fetch     (!)
XX  what is in 1.txt       -> UNKNOWN
XX  tell me what 1.txt contains -> UNKNOWN
```

**write_file: 7/15**
```
OK  write X to / save X to / make a file …containing / write the text …into / create …with content / save the following to / append …to
XX  create a file 1.txt with hello  -> UNKNOWN
XX  put hello in 1.txt              -> UNKNOWN
XX  set the contents of 1.txt to hello -> read_file  (!)
XX  store / output / echo hello into 1.txt -> UNKNOWN / TEXT
XX  create file 1.txt / new file 1.txt with hello -> UNKNOWN
```

**shell exec: 12/20** — see #749 for the full breakdown (date/env/stat/ln + delete/remove/copy/move all fail).

## Evidence — multilingual parity is largely missing

**"list files in this folder" across languages: 1/9**
```
OK  列出当前目录的文件 (zh)
XX  покажи файлы в этой папке / список файлов / какие файлы в этой папке (ru) -> UNKNOWN / write_program
XX  इस फ़ोल्डर में फ़ाइलें दिखाएँ (hi) -> not routed
XX  显示这个文件夹里的文件 (zh, other phrasing) -> web_search  (!)
XX  muestra los archivos… (es) / liste die dateien… (de) / liste les fichiers… (fr) -> UNKNOWN
```

**web_search across languages: 5/7** — en/ru/hi/zh work; **es/fr fail** (only en/ru/hi/zh are advertised — see #706 for growing beyond them).

## Expected (the generalization mandate)

For **every** intent/action the engine exposes (web_fetch, web_search, shell exec, read, write, edit, list, delete, move, copy, git, run-tests, build, install, calculate, …):

1. **Intent, not phrasing.** Resolve by the underlying intent using data-driven meanings/synonym sets in Links Notation, so **10–20+ natural variations per language** map to the same tool. New phrasings should be added **data-only** (no per-phrase handler), per the vision.
2. **No cross-tool misroutes.** `read/view/display/load/cat <file>` must never go to web_fetch/web_search; a bare URL must prefer web_fetch; "search the code" is grep, not web search (see #749 comment).
3. **Never silently `UNKNOWN`** for a clearly actionable request that maps to an available tool. If truly ambiguous, ask or pick the best candidate via the Universal Problem-Solving Algorithm — don't drop to "I could not determine…".
4. **Language parity.** The same intent resolves identically across **all supported/added languages** (en/ru/hi/zh today, plus any added by the data-only mechanism of #706). No language should be a second-class citizen where English routes and its exact translation returns UNKNOWN.
5. **Verify with a variation matrix in CI** (≥10–20 phrasings × each language × each intent), driven end-to-end through the real CLIs/TUIs (codex, agent, gemini, qwen, opencode, grok, cursor — claude via `-p` for spot checks; excluded from the required sweep), tracked under #671. A phrasing regression should fail CI.

## Repro
```
formal-ai serve --port 8080 --agent-mode &
for p in "read https://example.com" "grab https://example.com" "display 1.txt" "load 1.txt" "покажи файлы в этой папке" "set the contents of 1.txt to hello"; do
  curl -s localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
   -d "{\"model\":\"formal-ai\",\"tools\":[<web_fetch,web_search,exec_command,read_file,write_file>],\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"$p\"}]}" \
   | jq -r '.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0].function.name // "UNKNOWN/TEXT"'
done
```

## Scope note
This supersedes the original narrow "web_fetch phrasing" framing (residual of #712/#680) and generalizes it to **all** intents and **all** languages. Per-tool specifics live in: #749 (shell), #755 (read/write schema), #746 (web execution). Cross-cutting acceptance criteria (all languages, all tools) apply.

## Out-of-box common-pattern coverage (non-tool intents) — v0.297.1 re-test

Modern assistants are expected to handle these directly. Mixed results:
```
OK  what is 25 * 17 -> 425 ;  calculate 2^10 -> 1024 ;  capital of France -> Paris
OK  write a python function to reverse a string / write hello world in rust  (code-gen works)
XX  explain what a binary search is        -> UNKNOWN
XX  summarize: <text>                       -> echoes the text, does not summarize
XX  translate hello to Spanish             -> returns "hello" (no translation)
XX  fix this code: def f(: pass            -> UNKNOWN
```
`explain`, `summarize`, `translate`, and `fix/refactor code` are core capabilities that should work out of the box and generalize across 10-20+ phrasings and all languages.


---
## Implementation spec — generalization mechanism
1. **Synonym/intent sets in seed data** (Links Notation), per capability, per language — e.g. web_fetch verbs {fetch, get, download, open, load, retrieve, show, grab, pull, read (when a URL is the object), summarize, check, visit, browse, view} and their translations. Adding a phrasing = adding a data row, no code (per the vision).
2. **Object-type disambiguation:** if the object is a **URL** → web_fetch; a **local path/filename** → read_file (never web_fetch/web_search); a **search phrase** → web_search; "in the code/repo/files" → grep. Encode as rules over the parsed object type, not the verb alone.
3. **Never silent UNKNOWN:** when intent maps to an available capability, act; when genuinely ambiguous, use the Universal Problem-Solving Algorithm to pick the best candidate or ask via `request_user_input` — do not emit "I could not determine…".
4. **Test oracle:** for each capability, a fixture list of ≥15-20 phrasings × each language, asserting the right capability fires. Track counts (today: web_fetch 10/18, web_search 12/16, read 9/15, write 7/15, shell 12/20, multilingual list-files 1/9) and require them to reach 100%.
title: Generalize intent routing: 10-20+ NL variations per intent per language, no cross-tool misroutes, no silent UNKNOWN (supersedes web_fetch phrasing; v0.297.1)

Объект (часть объекта), обладающий симметриями. Запоминать их не нужно. Нужно научится отыскивать симметрии. Проблема в том, что для AGI научиться отыскивать симметрии нужно в самом общем случае, какой только возможен. Вплоть до преобразований Лоренца, калибровочной симметрии и дальше. В случае ARC набор симметрий ограничен двухмерной геометрией и перестановками. Если кто-то напряжётся и создаст специализированную систему, которая будет их решать, то не факт что это будет AGI. Обратное верно: система, которая их не проходит, AGI не является.

We need to do it based on our assiative deduplication as first step for any pattern inference.

https://github.com/linksplatform/Data.Doublets.Sequences/tree/main/csharp/Platform.Data.Doublets.Sequences/Converters - here you can find some deduplication/compression algorithms that are actually worked in C# on top of links storage, we need also to make sure we have implementation of them on top of Rust, including all dependencies like ways to initialize unique symbols, sequences and so on. For that we can deeply research https://github.com/linksplatform/Data.Doublets.Sequences for all necessary logic, we reimplement Rust sequences support in this repository, and later we will move all universal logic to separate components/repositories.

Previous pattern drafts:
- https://github.com/konard/LinksPlatform/issues/110
